# Chapter 6. The Surplus Value of the Small Master and the Surplus Value of the Capitalist

```{=html}
<p class="fst">If we take the value of labour-power, and correspondingly the labour time necessary for the maintenance of the worker, as given, then with the rate of surplus value the <em>mass</em> of surplus value which the individual worker supplies is also already determined. If the value of labour-power is 3 marks, and the rate of surplus value equal to 100 per cent, then the mass of surplus value which he produces is equal to 3 marks. But how large is the total mass of surplus value that accrues to a capitalist under given circumstances? Let us assume he employs 300 workers under the conditions cited above. Let the variable capital which he expends daily be equal to 900 marks, and the rate of surplus value 100 per cent. Its mass will then likewise amount to 900 marks daily. “The mass of surplus value produced is equal to the magnitude of the variable capital advanced, multiplied by the rate of surplus value.”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If one of these factors decreases, the mass of surplus value can be kept at the same level by increasing the other. Conversely, the increase of the one permits a corresponding reduction of the other, without any change in the mass of surplus value. A few examples may make this clear. Let a capitalist employ 300 workers; let the necessary labour time amount to 6 hours, the value of labour-power 8 marks; the daily labour time 12 hours. The mass of surplus value produced daily will be equal to 900 marks. Let the pliability of the workers allow the capitalist to raise the labour time to 15 hours. The rate of surplus value now amounts, other circumstances being equal, to 150 per cent,</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><u>           9 hours surplus labour         </u><br/> 6 hours necessary labour  .</p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">In order to produce the same mass of surplus value (900 marks) as before, the capitalist now no longer needs to advance 900 marks of variable capital, but only 600; instead of 300 workers, 200 now suffice.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But if the workers are not pliable, if on the contrary they, say through a particularly successful strike, enforce the reduction of the labour time from 12 to 9 hours, then the rate of surplus value will amount to only 50 per cent</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><u>           3 hours surplus labour         </u><br/> 6 hours necessary labour  .</p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">In order to produce the same mass of surplus value as before, the capitalist must now employ 600 workers, advance 1800 marks of variable capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>That the former case is the more agreeable to him we hardly need to emphasise. The capitalist strives to increase the mass of surplus value as much as possible; but it suits him better to attain this by increasing the rate of surplus value than by increasing the variable capital, by increasing the number of workers employed.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The rate of surplus value cannot, however, be determined arbitrarily; under given circumstances it is a more or less definite magnitude. If the rate of surplus value is taken as given, the production of a certain mass of surplus value requires the employment of a definite quantity of variable capital, which produces it, and of a definite quantity of constant capital, which absorbs it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This circumstance has become of historical significance.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Even before the development of capitalism, wage labourers were employed who produced surplus value. This was notably the case in the guild handicraft. But the number of workers whom a mediaeval handicraft master employed was small, and correspondingly small too was the mass of surplus value which the master pocketed. As a rule it did not suffice to grant him a suitable income; he had to lend a hand himself; the “small” master is no wage labourer and yet no capitalist either: an intermediate thing between the two.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If the employer of wage labourers was to become a real capitalist, then he had to employ so many workers that the mass of surplus value produced by them not only secured him a “station-appropriate” maintenance, but also allowed him to increase his wealth constantly, which under the capitalist mode of production is a necessity for him, as we shall yet see.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Not every sum of money enables its possessor to become a capitalist. If a possessor of money is to become an industrial capitalist, his stock of money must be large enough to be able to purchase a sufficient number of labour-powers and means of production going beyond the measure of the handicraft enterprise. But the possessor of money must also produce free of all obstacles which would forbid him to raise the number of his workers up to and beyond the necessary measure. The guild system of the Middle Ages sought to prevent the transformation of handicraft masters into capitalists by very greatly restricting the number of wage labourers whom a single master was allowed to employ.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">“It was the merchant who became the principal of the modern (capitalist) workshop, and not the old guild master.” (<strong>The Poverty of Philosophy</strong>, p. 135)</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The guild master is an appropriator of surplus value, but not yet a complete capitalist.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The guild journeyman is a producer of surplus value, but not yet a complete proletarian wage labourer.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The guild master still works himself. The capitalist is merely commander and overseer of the labour of others.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The guild journeyman is still a user of the means of production; they exist for his sake, in order to make his work possible and to ease it for him. He is the assistant, the fellow-worker of the master, and as a rule he wishes and is able one day to become a master himself.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The wage labourer of the capitalist mode of production, by contrast, is the sole worker in the production process, the source of surplus value, and the capitalist is the one who pumps it out of him. The means of production now serve above all the purpose of absorbing the labour-power of the worker into themselves: <em>it is now they that make use of the worker,</em> who in fact can never become a capitalist. The means of labour are no longer there to <em>ease</em> the worker's toil; they now help to <em>fetter</em> him to it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us glance into a capitalist factory: there we perhaps see thousands of spindles, thousands of hundredweights of cotton. They have all been bought in order to valorise themselves, that is, in order to absorb surplus value. But they do not valorise themselves without the addition of labour, and so they call for labour and yet more labour. The spinning machine is not there to ease the worker's work; rather, the spinner is there so that the spinning machine may valorise itself. The spindles run and demand human labour-power: the worker is hungry, but the spindle keeps working, and so he must gulp down his midday meal while he serves his mistress. His strength grows weary, he wants to sleep, but the spindles run on fresh and lively and clamour for more labour; and because the spindle runs, the worker too must not sleep.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The dead tool has subjugated the living worker.</p>
```


---

[↑ Previous: Chapter 5. The Working Day](ab2-kap05.qmd) [↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd) [Next: Chapter 7. Relative Surplus Value ↓](ab2-kap07.qmd)
